Days        Hours       Minutes     Seconds

🦋  Start Your Summer with Butterflies

Summer brings a surge of butterflies—monarchs, painted ladies, tiger swallowtails, and more. These vibrant pollinators thrive in protected landscapes, creating unforgettable seasonal displays. From coast to coast, here are TPL-protected places where butterflies abound.

FOLLOW THE FLUTTER
Photograph by Photographer

📰 TPL in the News

E&E News by Politico: Most Americans Back Funding for the Massive Park Maintenance Backlog, Survey Finds

FacilitiesNet: Sustainable K-12 Schoolyards Grow in Popularity

Sky-Hi News: Outdoor Organization Encourages Kids to Take Their Brains Outside

Deseret News: The Start of a Movement? How Time Outside Can Alter Our Nation's Health

Join Our Advisory Board

Advisory board members are TPL advocates, ambassadors, and donors who help increase our impact through philanthropy, advocacy, connections, and strategic guidance.

Join Our Team

Come join a passionate team working toward a mission you believe in. View all open positions.

Was this email forwarded to you? Sign up here.

More Stories for You

Seattle Makes the Top 10 List in the ParkScore Index

We released our 15th annual ParkScore index yesterday, and Seattle ranks #8 nationwide based on strong measurements for acreage, access, investment, amenities, and equity. Although the Emerald City fell out of the top 10 in 2020, it came back to the top 10 list in 2021 and has stayed there ever since.

 
 
 

Were the topics in this newsletter important?

  Very important

  Somewhat important

  Not important
 
 
 

Can You Guess Which Midwest City Ranks in the Nation's Top 5 Park Systems?
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL
LABEL

Don't feel like guessing? Reveal the answer.

CREEK QUIZ: GUESS WHO’S BACK

🐟 Which species of trout returned to Hazle Creek after a century-long absence?

A. Cutthroat

B. Brook

C. Tiger

Uncover which species made this miraculous comeback.

POP QUIZ

How many miles of creeks and headwaters are protected in Tualatin Mountain Forest?
10
15
20+

People. Nature. Big Ideas

Episode 24: Sweet Auburn's Enduring Legacy

Land carries meaning—and so do the places where history happened. In that sense, some buildings are physical archives that we can stand in and experience, connecting us to stories of human resilience. This episode is produced from Atlanta's Prince Hall Masonic Lodge, home to the former WERD studios, the first Black-owned U.S. radio station, exploring how self-determination shaped a movement—and can help shape the future. Listen now!

Listen where you stream
your favorite podcasts!
 

It's easy to send your comment—
just follow these simple steps:

1  Share your name.

And if you like, where you live or what parks you love.

2  Thank the National Park Service.

Let them know you appreciate the chance to weigh in.

3  Say why this matters to you.

Explain why it's essential that park interpretation reflects all of America's history—and why strong staffing and funding are needed to make that possible.

4  Add your personal touch.

Share a meaningful park memory or experience—something that helped you learn or feel connected.

5  Close with your request.

Call on the NPS to research, preserve, and share all aspects of our history—and to fund this work so these stories can be told.
SUBMIT A COMMENT

This button will take you to Regulations.gov, the federal site where you can send your comment straight to the National Park Service.
 
 

1  Your photo(s) should capture how you experience a public outdoor space—like a park, trail, schoolyard, or protected landscape.

2  You can enter daily with a new photo through February 9. Let them know you appreciate the chance to weigh in.

3  TPL will select the top five photos. You'll then have the chance to vote for your favorite finalist from February 11-15. Watch for the voting announcement on February 11.

4  All five finalists will receive a TPL prize package, and the one with the most votes will also win a TPL x ENO Giving Back hammock and a one-year AllTrails Plus membership.
 

Here's what's inspiring our TPL birders:

Keith Maley: “When I was four years old, I saw my spark bird. It was a ring-necked pheasant (shown above) under my neighbor Mary's bird feeder. I am obsessed with their colors, size, and tameness.”

Ronda Lee Chapman: “My favorite bird is the northern flicker (shown above). They are abundant in Denver and visit me often throughout my days. I love their call and how beautiful they are.”

Taj Schottland: “When I was four years old, I saw my spark bird. It was a ring-necked pheasant (shown above) under my neighbor Mary's bird feeder. I am obsessed with their colors, size, and tameness.”

Jeff Danter: “My favorite TPL-protected birding spot is the Chattahoochee RiverLands. This green oasis that runs through the heart of suburban Atlanta is a natural migration corridor that fills with the songs of hundreds of species every spring.”
Photos Flickr: Oregon Department of Fish & Wildlife, USFWS Mountain-Prairie, Mary Shattock, Shawn Taylor

IN THE SPOTLIGHT

Guess Who's Finding Their Happiness Outside More than Ever? (It Might Surprise You!) 🤔
AGES 13-17
AGES 25-34
AGES 45-54

Start My Annual Gift
 

[[S1:first_name:Friend]], you're invited!

The Great Northern Festival

The Main Cinema
115 SE Main Street
Minneapolis, MN 55414

❄️ Minnesota winters are long, but they also offer rare moments to pause, reflect, and reconnect. The Great Northern Festival embraces that spirit, using winter as a backdrop for meaningful gatherings that invite people to think differently about how we build community and inspire outdoor joy.

DONATE

Connecting everyone to the outdoors. We create parks and protect public land where they're needed most so that everyone will have access to the benefits and joys of the outdoors for generations to come.
